title: MCP & A2A
description: Model Context Protocol, tool integration, agent-to-agent communication, discovery, governance, and security.

questions.title: What is MCP and what problem does it solve?
questions.scenario: A platform engineering group runs agent ecosystem that exposes enterprise tools through MCP and coordinates specialist agents. A recent review found that tools are connected faster than security, versioning, and audit controls mature; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for MCP and what problem does it solve measured by tool-call success, authorization failures, audit completeness, and integration reuse.
questions.expectedAnswer: MCP and what problem does it solve is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In agent ecosystem that exposes enterprise tools through MCP and coordinates specialist agents, the practical approach is MCP and what problem does it solve applied with clear problem framing, measurable validation, operational controls, and production feedback. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from tool manifests, resource metadata, auth scopes, audit logs, and protocol versions, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using domain datasets, baselines, monitoring, evaluation dashboards, and human review, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is generic implementation that cannot be measured or operated; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use tool-call success, authorization failures, audit completeness, and integration reuse, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to generic implementation that cannot be measured or operated. MCP and what problem does it solve addresses that risk through MCP and what problem does it solve applied with clear problem framing, measurable validation, operational controls, and production feedback, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using tool-call success, authorization failures, audit completeness, and integration reuse.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from tool manifests, resource metadata, auth scopes, audit logs, and protocol versions. Implement the change with domain datasets, baselines, monitoring, evaluation dashboards, and human review, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in agent ecosystem that exposes enterprise tools through MCP and coordinates specialist agents, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds MCP and what problem does it solve applied with clear problem framing, measurable validation, operational controls, and production feedback; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor tool-call success, authorization failures, audit completeness, and integration reuse so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

relatedQuestions.title: What security controls matter for MCP tool integration?

commonMistakes: Explaining MCP and what problem does it solve as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.

Ignoring the main risk: generic implementation that cannot be measured or operated.

questions.realWorldExample: A platform engineering group applied MCP and what problem does it solve in agent ecosystem that exposes enterprise tools through MCP and coordinates specialist agents. The baseline suffered from generic implementation that cannot be measured or operated, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented MCP and what problem does it solve applied with clear problem framing, measurable validation, operational controls, and production feedback using domain datasets, baselines, monitoring, evaluation dashboards, and human review, then evaluated the change against tool-call success, authorization failures, audit completeness, and integration reuse. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for MCP and what problem does it solve, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: What is agent-to-agent communication?
questions.scenario: A platform engineering group runs agent ecosystem that exposes enterprise tools through MCP and coordinates specialist agents. A recent review found that tools are connected faster than security, versioning, and audit controls mature; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for agent-to-agent communication measured by tool-call success, authorization failures, audit completeness, and integration reuse.
questions.expectedAnswer: agent-to-agent communication is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In agent ecosystem that exposes enterprise tools through MCP and coordinates specialist agents, the practical approach is bounded planning, typed tool calls, state management, approval gates, and trace-based evaluation. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from tool manifests, resource metadata, auth scopes, audit logs, and protocol versions, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using LangGraph, tool schemas, ServiceNow/Jira APIs, and LangSmith traces, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is unbounded autonomy, tool misuse, loops, and hidden state corruption; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use tool-call success, authorization failures, audit completeness, and integration reuse, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.
